data.title: Sector Update: Financial
data.description:
Financial stocks were mixed late Friday afternoon trading, with the NYSE Financial Index up 0.1% and the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF) shedding 0.2%.

The Philadelphia Housing Index declined 0.4%, and the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) added 0.1%.

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) declined 2.4% to $108,968, and the yield for 10-year US Treasuries fell 4 basis points to 4.51%.

In economic news, President Donald Trump said he's recommending a "straight" 50% tariff on the EU, effective June 1, because the bloc has been "very difficult to deal with." The EU is the US's largest trading partner, accounting for 18% of total trade, a Stifel note said.

New home sales in the US unexpectedly rose in April despite an increase in median prices, government data showed Friday. Single-family home sales rose 10.9% sequentially to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 743,000 units last month, according to the Census Bureau and the Department of Housing and Urban Development. The Bloomberg-polled consensus was for a 4% decline based on the unrevised March print.

In sector news, major US banks are in talks over the potential issuance of a joint stablecoin, which serves as a digital dollar in cryptocurrency markets, The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday. The talks have involved companies co-owned by Citigroup (C), Wells Fargo (WFC), Bank of America (BAC), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and other major commercial banks so far, the report said.

In corporate news, Kingstone shares jumped past 8% after the company's chief executive disclosed the acquisition of 5,000 company shares in a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

data.title: US Supreme Court temporarily halts access sought by watchdog group to DOGE records
data.description:
Key points:
  • Watchdog group sought records on DOGE's operations
  • Lower court found DOGE is likely subject to FOIA law

The U.S. Supreme Court on Friday temporarily paused judicial orders requiring the Department of Government Efficiency, established by President Donald Trump and spearheaded by his billionaire adviser Elon Musk, to turn over records and answer questions in the coming days and weeks concerning its operations.

The court put on hold Washington-based U.S. District Judge Christopher Cooper's orders for DOGE to respond to a government watchdog group's requests for information after finding that DOGE likely is a government agency covered by the federal Freedom of Information Act.

The Supreme Court's action, called an administrative stay, gives it additional time to consider the Justice Department's formal request to block Cooper's order while litigation proceeds in a lower court.

Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW) sued, seeking an array of records from DOGE through the FOIA statute, a law that allows the public to seek access to records produced by government agencies.

The group sought information on DOGE's activities over its role in the mass firings and cuts to federal programs pursued after the Republican president returned to office in January.

The Trump administration contends that DOGE is an advisory entity and not subject to FOIA. In response, CREW sought information to determine whether DOGE is subject to FOIA because it wields the kind of authority of an agency independent of the president.

Cooper ruled in April that DOGE had to turn over some records sought by CREW and was entitled to question DOGE's Acting Administrator Amy Gleason at a deposition. A federal appeals court declined on May 14 to put Cooper's order on hold.

The administration urged the Supreme Court to act, saying that the judge's orders intrude on the powers of the executive branch and compromise the ability of a wide array of advisers to provide candid, confidential advice to the president.

In one of his decisions, Cooper said DOGE's operations have been marked by "unusual secrecy." In another, the judge said the language of Trump's executive orders concerning DOGE suggests that it is "exercising substantial independent authority."

data.title: Trouble with AI 'hallucinations' spreads to big law firms
data.description:
Another large law firm was forced to explain itself to a judge this week for submitting a court filing with made-up citations generated by an artificial intelligence chatbot.

Attorneys from Mississippi-founded law firm Butler Snow apologized to U.S. District Judge Anna Manasco in Alabama after they inadvertently included case citations generated by ChatGPT in two court filings.

Butler Snow partner Matthew Reeves said in a Monday filing that he regretted his "lapse in diligence and judgment" for failing to verify the citations.

The 400-lawyer firm, which did not immediately respond to a request for comment, is defending former Alabama Department of Corrections Commissioner Jeff Dunn in an inmate's lawsuit alleging he was repeatedly attacked in prison. Dunn has denied wrongdoing. The judge has not yet said whether she will impose sanctions over the filings.

Jamila Mensah of Norton Rose Fulbright, one of the lawyers representing plaintiff Frankie Johnson, declined to comment.

AI-generated fictions, known as "hallucinations," have cropped up in court filings and landed attorneys in hot water ever since ChatGPT and other generative AI programs became widely available more than two years ago. Courts have sanctioned and admonished attorneys around the country for violating professional rules that require them to vet their work however it is produced.

Many of the cases have involved small law firms or self-represented litigants. But examples of big firms or big companies grappling with AI hallucinations are growing.

Last week a lawyer at law firm Latham & Watkins, which is defending AI company Anthropic in a copyright lawsuit related to music lyrics, apologized to a California federal judge after submitting an expert report that cited an article title invented by AI.

Lawyers for the music publishers suing Anthropic have asked the judge to exclude the report. The judge has not yet ruled on the request.

Earlier this month, a court-appointed special master imposed sanctions and ordered law firm K&L Gates and a smaller firm, Ellis George, to pay $31,100 for what he called a "collective debacle" in which they included inaccurate case citations and quotations stemming from the use of AI in a filing.

Lawyers from the two firms are representing former Los Angeles County District Attorney Jackie Lacey in a dispute with insurance giant State Farm. A spokesperson for K&L Gates and a lawyer at Ellis George did not immediately respond to requests for comment. State Farm and its lawyers at Sheppard Mullin did not immediately respond to similar requests.

The special master, retired judge Michael Wilner, wrote in the order that he had been "affirmatively misled" by the filing. "I read their brief, was persuaded (or at least intrigued) by the authorities that they cited, and looked up the decisions to learn more about them – only to find that they didn't exist," he wrote. "That's scary."

Daniel Linna, a senior lecturer and director of law and technology initiatives at Northwestern's law and engineering schools, said the inclusion of AI-generated fabrications in court briefs is in part a result of a lack of education and training.

Despite the perils, AI also has the potential to increase the quality of legal briefs and improve access to justice, he said.

data.title: Gilead Sciences Officer Sells Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameMercier Johanna
RelationshipChief Commercial Officer
TypeSell
Amount$3,010,563
SEC FilingForm 4

Johanna Mercier, Chief Commercial Officer of Gilead Sciences, sold 28,000 shares of common stock on May 22, 2025, under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan. The sales were conducted at prices ranging from $107.06 to $107.95, with the total sale amounting to $3,010,563. Following these transactions, Mercier directly owns 121,152 shares of Gilead Sciences.

SEC Filing: GILEAD SCIENCES, INC. [ GILD ] - Form 4 - May. 23, 2025

data.title: Rocket Cos., Middleby, Diebold Nixdorf, and More Stocks See Action From Activist Investors — Barrons.com
data.description:
These disclosures are from 13Ds filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission. 13Ds are filed within 10 days of an entity's attaining more than 5% in any class of a company's securities. Subsequent changes in holdings or intentions must be reported in amended filings. This material is from May 15 through May 21, 2025. Source: VerityData

Original Filings

Middleby (MIDD) Garden Investment Management disclosed a new position in the food-service-equipment maker of 3,139,834 shares. Garden Investment purchased 503,968 of those shares from May 9 through May 16, 2025, at per share prices ranging from $138.87 to $149.81. Following those purchases, Garden Investment owns 5.9% of Middleby's outstanding stock. Shares of Middleby have gained about 9.1% since the beginning of this year.

Rocket Cos. (RKT) ValueAct Capital disclosed a new position in the financial-services company of 15,125,609 shares. ValueAct purchased 9,160,900 of those shares from March 27 through May 15, 2025, at per share prices ranging from $11.30 to $13.74. Following those purchases, ValueAct owns 9.9% of Rocket's outstanding stock. Shares of Rocket have gained roughly 12.8% since the beginning of this year.

Decreases in Holdings

Diebold Nixdorf (DBD) Hein Park Capital Management lowered its stake in the financial and retail technology company to 1,542,511 shares. Hein Park did so through the sale of 490,593 Diebold Nixdorf shares from March 3 through May 16, 2025, at per share prices ranging from $42 to $49.54. Following the latest sales, Hein Park continues to own 4.1% of Diebold Nixdorf's outstanding stock, placing Hein Park below the 5% threshold that would require it to report further sales. Shares of Diebold Nixdorf have gained roughly 12.4% since the start of this year.

NextDecade (NEXT) Valinor Management reduced its stake in the liquid-natural-gas producer to 12,993,013 shares. Valinor did so through the sale of 1,074,544 NextDecade shares from March 18 through May 13, 2025, at per share prices ranging from $8.25 to $9.71. Following the latest sales, Valinor continues to own 4.98% of NextDecade's outstanding stock, placing Valinor below the 5% threshold that would require it to report further sales. Shares of NextDecade have lost about 12.8% of their value since the start of this year.

Gulfport Energy (GPOR) Silver Point Capital lowered its stake in the natural-gas-weighted exploration-and-production company to 3,690,252 shares. Silver Point did so through the sale of 610,000 Gulfport shares on May 13, 2025, for $194.84 apiece, then offset those sales through the purchase of 76,986 shares for the same price that day. Following the latest activity, Silver Point continues to own 19% of Gulfport's outstanding stock. Shares of Gulfport have gained about 5% since the start of this year.

BridgeBio Pharma (BBIO) KKR Genetic Disorder reduced its stake in the biotechnology company to 13,260,971 shares. KKR Genetic Disorder did so through the sale of six million BridgeBio shares through an open market block sale on May 12, 2025, at a price of $34.20 per share. Following the sale, KKR Genetic Disorder continues to own 7% of BridgeBio's outstanding stock. Shares of BridgeBio have gained roughly 16.5% since the start of this year.

Email: editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Corn Futures Fall After Trump Threatens EU With Tariff - Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Corn for July delivery fell 0.9%, to $4.59 a bushel, on the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday, leading grains lower after President Trump made a threat to institute a 50% on goods from the European Union.
  • Soybeans for July delivery fell 0.6%, to $10.60 3/4 a bushel.
  • Wheat for July delivery fell 0.2%, to $5.43 1/2 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Tough Talk: President Trump's new tariff threat via Truth Social that targeted the EU was the big news event that provided pressure for agricultural futures. "Beans were quiet on the overnight, then the tariff news about Europe hit the newswire, and beans dropped to 10 cents lower," says Naomi Blohm of Total Farm Marketing. Other analysts said that Friday's news spurred profit-taking by traders.

Stopping Point: The Memorial Day weekend is interrupting a strong run for crop commodities. Soybeans finished higher for 9 out of the past 11 sessions, while wheat closed with gains 6 out of the past 8 sessions. The protectiveness that traders operated with today has sapped that momentum, with any unpredictable swerves that come over the long weekend potentially keeping the momentum off when trading starts next week.

INSIGHT

Growing Waterlogged: Rainfall is very welcome in the western half of the U.S. Corn Belt, but in eastern states precipitation has grown excessive--and milder temperatures are slowing down the evaporation of this moisture, according to the USDA in its daily weather forecast. "Cool weather throughout the Midwest is slowing evaporation rates in the wake of recent rainfall, limiting opportunities for large-scale fieldwork," the agency said. The USDA also projects that storms are expected to travel through many growing areas over the Memorial Day holiday weekend.

Prepping for a Slog: ING Economics said in a note that "it has always been clear that the U.S. administration's stance on the EU will be different than with most other trading partners." Trump's Truth Social post calling for a new 50% tariff on EU shipments has given safe haven futures like gold a boost, while other agricultural futures are mostly underwater. Traders are nervous that the new spat will escalate, although many investors are unconvinced that Trump will follow through with threats, or at least won't for an extended period of time. "Anything is possible, from a potential agreement to further escalation," said ING.

AHEAD

  • The USDA and the CBOT will be closed in observance of Memorial Day on Monday. Both will reopen Tuesday.
  • The USDA will release its weekly Crop Progress report at 4 p.m. ET Monday.
  • Ethanol producer REX American Resources will release its Q1 2025 earnings at 7:30 a.m. ET Wednesday.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Brazil government was agile to change part of IOF tax hike, says central bank chief
data.description:
Brazil's central bank chief Gabriel Galipolo cheered the finance ministry's decision to reverse part of its move to hike the IOF tax on some financial transactions before markets opened on Friday, saying it was "adequate".

"We need to recognize the agility with which the ministry acted it the end," Galipolo told an event hosted by think tank FGV, saying that the reaction of local markets reflected that.

Brazil's currency weakened over 1% against the U.S. dollar (BRBY) in early Friday trading, but later pared nearly all losses amid global dollar weakness (.DXY.).

The Finance Ministry late on Thursday scrapped part of higher IOF tax on financial transactions it had announced earlier in the day, after critics both inside and outside the government blasted some of the measures as backsliding toward the return of capital controls.

It was clear to markets that the government's goal with the IOF hike was to increase revenues to meet its fiscal target, Galipolo said, adding however that he personally disagreed with the move due to concerns about its potential impact on the foreign exchange rate and Brazil's capital account.

The central bank head noted that the government had more challenges than the monetary authority to seek its goals due to the need for political negotiations.

In his remarks, the governor added that the central bank was at a moment of more caution and flexibility in its monetary policy, meaning the authority should not do sudden interest rate moves given the high level of uncertainty.

Earlier this month, the bank raised its benchmark interest rate to 14.75%, the highest in nearly 20 years, and dropped any forward guidance.

data.title: House of the Week: A Pair of Historic Homes in New Orleans — WSJ
data.description:
By E.B. Solomont

Ken Fulk is a New Orleans habitué, who has been inspired by the city's music, culture and architecture. In 2021, the designer, who splits his time between San Francisco, New York and Provincetown, Mass., jumped at the chance to buy and restore two homes built in the 1800s in the city's French Quarter.

Fulk, 60, paid $2.45 million for the main house and $1.58 million for the guest house, both previously owned by hoteliers Frances and Rodney Smith. The Smiths also owned the nearby Soniat House Hotel, where Fulk was a regular during his trips to New Orleans.

When the Smiths listed the Soniat for sale, Fulk bought the hotel and several nearby buildings, including the two homes." It was a kismet moment," he says. "It was almost like, 'How could we not do this?'"

High style and provenance

Built around 1854, the main residence has a white stone staircase from Italy with French ironwork. The Smiths had tapped British designer Nicky Haslam, who added imported wood, opulent chandeliers and hand-painted wallpaper.

In 2009, they sold the home to late Microsoft co-founder Paul Allen, records show. Allen added a ground-floor recording studio, Fulk says. The guest house, built around 1843, has historic windows and detailed millwork.

A careful restoration

Fulk says his goal was to restore Haslam's work. "Part of the mission was, 'Don't screw it up,'" he says.

The work involved refinishing and cleaning up surfaces like wood in a powder bathroom that he says was imported from Versailles and hand-painted wallpaper in the parlor with hidden animals in a gilded, floral motif. He also tracked down furniture that was previously in the house. "It was more about preservation than anything," he says.

Can't-miss features

The main residence has a wood-paneled elevator and a secluded courtyard. In the salon, there is a 60-candle Italian chandelier.

Reason for selling?

Fulk has enjoyed entertaining at the house, but is now focused on redeveloping the Soniat. He relishes the idea of staying in the hotel's canopied beds and ordering room service instead. "I don't think I need a huge, three-story residence," he says.

In addition to the two renovated homes, Fulk listed two other buildings nearby, one asking $1.259 million and the other asking $1.18 million. The four properties could be turned into a compound.

Market snapshot

The homes are located in the French Quarter's residential section, where Brad Pitt and Angelina Jolie used to have a home, said Elizabeth McNulty of Latter & Blum | Compass, who is marketing the property with colleague Kristin Patterson. The median sale price in the neighborhood was $378,000 in April 2025, up 10.4% from the previous year, according to real-estate brokerage Redfin. Homes at the high end of the market are selling quickly, McNulty says. "Turn-key, furnished is definitely an ideal scenario coming to New Orleans."

Write to E.B. Solomont at eb.solomont@wsj.com

data.title: US Supreme Court temporarily halts access sought by watchdog group to DOGE records
data.description:
The U.S. Supreme Court on Friday temporarily paused judicial orders requiring the Department of Government Efficiency, established by President Donald Trump and spearheaded by his billionaire adviser Elon Musk, to turn over records and answer questions in the coming days and weeks concerning its operations.

The court put on hold Washington-based U.S. District Judge Christopher Cooper's orders for DOGE to respond to a government watchdog group's requests for information after finding that DOGE likely is a government agency covered by the federal Freedom of Information Act.

The Supreme Court's action, called an administrative stay, gives it additional time to consider the Justice Department's formal request to block Cooper's order while litigation proceeds in a lower court.

data.title: Bitcoin price ‘breather’ expected as short-term traders realize $11.6B in profit
data.description:
Key takeaways:

  • Short-term Bitcoin holders realized $11.6 billion in profit over the past 30 days, suggesting a potential pause or local top in the market.

  • Technical indicators show cooling momentum as retail investor sentiment falls to a 90-day low and liquidity data points to price volatility.

Bitcoin price recently hit a new all-time high of $111,800, but the bullish momentum may slow down as onchain data from Glassnode reveals significant profit-taking by short-term holders (STHs), potentially signaling a market "breather."

Glassnode analysis shows that STHs, often considered traders rather than long-term investors, have realized a staggering $11.6 billion in profits over the last 30 days. This follows a sharp rebound in Bitcoin’s price, pushing past the STH cost-basis of $93,000. The profit-taking peaked at $747 million daily, a rapid increase from the $1.2 billion realized in the last 30-day period, highlighting a shift in new investor sentiment. 

The STH Realized Profit/Loss Ratio has spiked, with profits now significantly outweighing losses, and only 8% of trading days have seen this ratio at a higher level.

This level of profit-taking is typical during bullish trends but often precedes local market tops. Excessive profit-taking can overwhelm new demand, creating overhead supply resistance and halt Bitcoin’s upward trajectory. 

Crypto analyst Axel Adler Jr noted that Bitcoin’s 30-day price momentum has already slowed by 38%, currently sitting at 19%. Adler described it as a “technical cooldown” after the recent peak. The Bitcoin researcher suggested the market needs a “breather” before potentially resuming its rally.

Similarly, analysis from Hyblock Capital advised caution as the previous three months outlined Bitcoin consistently targeting short liquidity zones above current prices, driving its recent highs. 

However, retail sentiment is at a 90-day low, with only 31.59% of retail accounts holding long positions. Meanwhile, open interest is at a 90-day high, and combined order books sit in the 91st percentile, signaling high liquidity and potential volatility.

Related: US Bitcoin ETFs near record month after $1.5B inflows in 2 days

Bitcoin open interest dropped by $1.2 billion as BTC fell under $110,000

Bitcoin experienced a sharp decline, dropping to $108,000 from $111,300 before the New York trading session opened on May 23. US President Donald Trump’s announcement of a 50% tariff on European Union imports, effective June 1, 2025, triggered the price dump, which sparked global market uncertainty.

The price plunge resulted in a significant $1.2 billion open interest reduction in Bitcoin positions, signaling a wave of deleveraging as traders reduced futures exposure.

Cointelegraph Markets & Research
@CointelegraphMT

🚨LATEST: #Bitcoin open interest exhibits a $1.2 billion position flush after $BTC drops below $110,000. pic.twitter.com/0ee46BiHGD

May 23, 2025

Despite the initial sell-off, Bitcoin rebounded above $109,000, with speculators dismissing the sell-off period. Regarding the current market trend, crypto trader Honey pointed out that any corrections could be potential buying opportunities. The trader said,

“As expected we pumped and now that the golden cross has happened on BTC, we generally see a market-wide pullback so I’d be cautious here. Dips are for buying.”

Related: Bitcoin price drops 4% as Trump EU tariff talk liquidates over $300M

This article does not contain investment advice or recommendations. Every investment and trading move involves risk, and readers should conduct their own research when making a decision.

data.title: Congo ex-President Kabila calls for immediate withdrawal of all foreign troops from territory
data.description:
Democratic Republic of Congo former President Joseph Kabila called for the immediate withdrawal of all foreign troops from the Central African country's national territory in a speech on Friday.

He also called the nation's justice system "an instrument of oppression for a dictatorship" in the speech.

data.title: World Moves Toward Bilateral Trade Relationships — Market Talk
data.description:
1516 ET - Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a TV interview Friday that the EU has a "collective-action problem," voicing frustration with U.S.-EU trade negotiations. Following a new deal between the U.K. and the EU governing post-Brexit relations, there's more evidence the world is moving toward a fragmented trade landscape hinging on individual bilateral deals, Cohen & Steers multi-asset head Jeff Palma tells WSJ. Over the past two months, a new bilateral deal between the U.S. and the U.K., and a tentative de-escalation with China following bilateral talks, have helped turn down the temperature on trade. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has said the White House is aiming for a slew of new bilateral deals in the months ahead. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1446 ET - Treasury yields fall today, but still end the week higher, as investors focus on the widening U.S. deficit and Trump revives tariff turbulence. Longer-term yields rise faster than the short end. U.S. markets closed Monday for Memorial Day. April durable goods orders and the Conference Board consumer confidence index are due Tuesday. GDP and jobless claims will come on Thursday. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the PCE, and the University of Michigan consumer survey are due Friday. The 30-year yield ends the week up 0.141 percentage, at 5.036%, while the 10-year rises 0.071 percentage point, to 4.508%. The two-year gains 0.005 p.p. to 3.985%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1440 ET [Dow Jones]--After releasing updates across its portfolio in the last few months, Wix.com's product offerings appear increasingly likely to improve customer conversion, retention and monetization over time, Baird analysts said in a research note. In addition to the latest features, including an AI visual design platform and an AI assistant, the company seems to have more on the horizon for later this year and beyond, the analysts said. Such drivers, as well as strong first-quarter results, add more credibility to the company's full-year outlook, which guides for sales growth of 12% to 14%, the analysts said. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1427 ET - Apple has sought to avoid the brunt of tariff pressures by shipping iPhone products to the US from India and Vietnam, but it hasn't been enough to placate President Trump. Trump said today iPhones manufactured abroad could face import taxes of at least 25%, noting he expects the devices to be made in the U.S. and not in China, India, or elsewhere. "Given the deal with China, Apple will have been hoping that tensions with the US government would begin to ease, but this has appeared to be a false dawn," says Quilter Cheviot's Ben Barringer. "Given the renewed rhetoric coming from Trump, this position may need to change, and greater consideration given to either upping prices, moving supply chains or ultimately both." (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1356 ET - McDonald's beverage oriented offshoot, CosMc's, will wind down later next month as it shifts to testing some of the novel drinks in its regular U.S. restaurants, the company says. McDonald's had roughly 10 CosMc's locations in Texas and Illinois that it began opening as a pilot in December 2023. The company said fewer customers asked for customized drinks at CosMc's than it expected, and it will try serving the drinks in its existing restaurants. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

1351 ET - Despite notching continued sales growth above expectations in its latest quarter, Workday investors appear unimpressed with the company's reaffirmed guidance as it tumbles 11%. The move "may be evidence of a material swing in investor expectations into the April quarter prints," UBS analysts say in a research note. Just a month ago, reaffirmed guidance could have driven a relief rally, they say, as investors feared a slowdown in corporate spending amid macroeconomic and tariff-related headwinds. Other software companies, like Palantir and Monday.com, have bumped up their full-year expectations in recent weeks after posting continued quarterly sales growth, relieving investors, and potentially raising the bar for similar companies.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1327 ET - With a $795 million modification, Palantir's US Army contract for its Maven Smart System is now worth up to about $1.3 billion, marking the company's first agreement to cross the billion-dollar threshold, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a research note. The deal adds further momentum for Palantir's federal business in North America, which will likely increase from President Trump's plans for an air and missile defense shield, the analysts say. With a NATO partnership also helping it gain traction in Europe, Palantir is in a sweet spot to benefit from governments in both continents going all-in on efficiency and AI aims, and will likely gain more federal deals going forward, the analysts say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1308 ET - Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says in a TV interview with Bloomberg that a regulatory reform aimed at improving conditions in the Treasury market could make progress soon. The reform would shift the supplementary leverage ratio to decrease the capital that banks must hold against portfolios of Treasury securities, making it less expensive for them to own U.S. government debt. "That is moving along very quickly between the three banking regulators, the Fed, the OCC and the FDIC, so I would think we could see something on that over the summer," Bessent says. He cites estimates that the reform could lower Treasury yields by tenths of a percentage point. "Banks are being penalized for holding Treasurys," Bessent says of the current regulatory regime. Some analysts counter that the reform wouldn't have a meaningful impact on the Treasury market. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1304 ET - Workday's unchanged outlook after logging higher than expected revenue last quarter, seems to be a conservatism move in the face of economic uncertainty, Bank of America Securities analysts say in a research note. "It is clear though that macro pressure is still unseen, suggesting potential for upside if the environment holds," the analysts say, suggesting the setup could help the company beat current guidance. Workday logged 13% subscription sales growth last quarter, beating expectations from both analysts and the company itself. Workday slides 11%. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1253 ET - Investments in sectors backed by real assets--areas such as real estate, infrastructure, utilities and energy--are likely to show more stability than the broader equity market amid policy-driven volatility, Cohen & Steers multi-asset head Jeff Palma tells WSJ. He adds the caveat that some classes of real assets, in particular real estate and infrastructure, remain sensitive to swings in interest rates. But the real-asset space nonetheless gives investors attractive diversification, Palma says. Year to date, the S&P utilities index has gained 5.9% and the S&P real-estate index is down 0.8%, compared with a 1.4% decline year to date in the broader S&P 500. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1229 ET - Bond markets' initial reaction to Trump's new tariff threats was to sell off Treasurys, pushing yields up from early lows, but it didn't go too far. Apparently, investors expect the steep 50% tariff proposal on imports from the E.U. to be negotiated down, similarly to what happened with China. "The market has kind of slowed down now because it's happened before, and we know what happened before," Vanguard's John Madziyire says. "It's kind of like the boy who cried wolf," he says. "The administration is not crazy, they have a plan and there is a limit," to how far they will go, he says. The 10-year Treasury yields is at 4.503%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1202 ET - Federal Reserve policymakers have been clear for months that they want more clarity about fiscal and trade policy, and on how the economy responds, before they take further action on interest rates. Over the past month, their patient approach has led traders to cancel bets that an interest-rate cut is possible at the Fed's June meeting, and traders now see the pause continuing through the July meeting as well. Bets in futures markets, however, reflect a better-than-even probability that rates will move lower by the end of September. In effect, it's a wager that the next four months will clear the air enough for the Fed to gain confidence--either that slowing inflation will allow for an easier stance, or that a deteriorating economy will require more accommodation. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Futures End Week Unchanged — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. natural gas futures recover ground to end the week flat as the low-demand shoulder season enters its last days. A Tuesday short-covering rally was followed by two days of losses, with the market awaiting summer weather to lift demand and temper the rate of inventory builds. "Overall, the market has shrugged off yesterday's large +120 Bcf injection, though longer-range weather forecasts have added heat which is set to dampen next week's injection with the market expecting a build near 100 Bcf," Gelber & Associates says in a note. Nymex natural gas settles up 2.5% at $3.334/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Sector Update: Energy
data.description:
Energy stocks rose late Friday afternoon with the NYSE Energy Sector Index and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) each adding 0.6%.

The Dow Jones US Utilities index gained 1.2%, and the Philadelphia Oil Service Sector index rose 0.4%.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil rose 0.9% to $61.74 a barrel, and global benchmark Brent advanced 0.9% to $64.99 a barrel. Henry Hub natural gas futures rose 2.5% to $3.34 per 1 million BTU.

In corporate news, President Donald Trump on Friday signed orders that seek to hasten the construction of nuclear power plants and help the US claim an edge in nuclear energy, according to media reports. Shares of Constellation Energy , Oklo , Nuscale Power , Uranium Energy , Energy Fuels , Centrus Energy and Nano Nuclear Energy gained.
